rubric_en: Critics wrote the work off as kitsch for the masses. But a set of murals celebrating Social Security—now threatened with destruction—show that such sweeping judgments went too far.
rubric_zh: 评论家曾把这些作品斥为“为大众量身定制的低俗艺术”。但一组歌颂社会保障的壁画——如今却濒临被毁——表明这种一刀切的定性走得太过了。

paragraphs.en: Ben Shahn’s mural on one wall of a corridor in the Cohen building portrays unemployed men before the advent of Social Security. On the opposite wall, he painted his vision of bustling life afterward. (U.S. General Services Administration)
paragraphs.zh: 本·尚（Ben Shahn）在科恩（Cohen）大楼一条走廊的一面墙上绘制了社会保障诞生前失业男子的景象；而在对面的墙上，他描绘了随后的繁忙生活场景。（美国总务管理局）

paragraphs.en: To forge a new social contract is one thing. To explain it to people is another. The bureaucrats of the New Deal understood that very well. They also knew that art and architecture could be powerful spreaders of political ideas. As it brought America out of the Depression, the Franklin D. Roosevelt administration built or funded courthouses, post offices, town halls, gyms, pools, auditoriums, and much more—tens of thousands of public buildings and facilities—and its arts programs employed as many as 10,000 artists to decorate them.
paragraphs.zh: 缔造一份新社会契约是一回事，将其向公众讲清楚又是另一回事。新政时期的官僚深知这一点。他们也明白，艺术与建筑都可以成为传播政治理念的强大工具。在帮助美国走出大萧条期间，富兰克林·德拉诺·罗斯福政府修建或资助了法院、大楼、邮局、市政厅、体育馆、游泳池、礼堂等大量公共建筑和设施，规模达到数万座，并通过其艺术计划雇佣了多达一万名艺术家为这些建筑作装饰。

paragraphs.en: The Wilbur J. Cohen Federal Building, originally the Social Security Board Building, exemplifies New Deal art and architecture at their best. It’s the “Sistine Chapel of the New Deal,” in the words of the founder of the Living New Deal, an organization dedicated to documenting and preserving the history and culture of the period. In 1935, Roosevelt’s Social Security Act changed the covenant between the American people and the state. Social Security enshrined a new right to be protected against economic vicissitudes, reversing the assumption that Americans would scrape by on the strength of “rugged individualism”—a phrase made popular by Roosevelt’s predecessor Herbert Hoover, who was being blamed for America’s woes. Seeking muralists and sculptors to work on the new building, the office in charge of the most prestigious commissions, the U.S. Treasury’s Section of Fine Arts, held competitions and published an essay that effectively provided the theme. Its title was “The Meaning of Social Security.”
paragraphs.zh: 威尔伯·J·科恩联邦大楼，最初名为社会保障委员会大楼，是新政艺术与建筑艺术成就的典范。它被“活生生的新政”（Living New Deal）这一致力于记录并保存这一时期历史与文化的组织创始人称为“新政的西斯廷教堂”。1935年，罗斯福的《社会保障法》改变了美国人民与国家之间的契约。社会保障确立了新权利，即保障个人免受经济波动之害，从而推翻了“坚韧的个人主义”这一观念；该术语曾因罗斯福前任赫伯特·胡佛而流行，人们正是因此将美国的困境归咎于他。在招募壁画家和雕塑家参与新大楼建设时，负责最具威望委托项目的机构——美国财政部的精美艺术局——举办了竞赛，并发表了一篇提供主题导向的论文，其题目是《社会保障的意义》。

paragraphs.en: The result is an uncommonly lovely New Deal mission statement. The Cohen building (it was renamed in 1988 for the first professional employee of the Social Security Board, who later became the secretary of health, education, and welfare) is a 1.2-million-square-foot edifice covering a city block across from the National Mall in Washington, D.C. The architect, Charles Z. Klauder, gave it strong, modernized classical lines and Egyptian Revival and Art Deco flourishes. It is a vision of big government rooted in the past and reaching toward the future. The interior proclaims the beauty of efficient bureaucracy. Once inside the door, the presumed public servant is led straight to bronze-clad elevators and escalators, and from there whisked to his duties. Green marble covers the walls, rounding corners with sinuous Art Moderne curves.
paragraphs.zh: 最终诞生的是一则出人意料地优美的新政宣言。科恩大楼（1988年以美国社会保障委员会首位职业雇员命名，他后来成为卫生、教育和福利部长）坐落于华盛顿特区国家广场对面的一个完整城市街区，占地120万平方英尺。建筑师查尔斯·Z·克劳德（Charles Z. Klauder）赋予其坚实而现代化的古典线条，并加入埃及复兴与装饰艺术（Art Deco）的装饰元素。它展现了一种扎根传统、面向未来的“大政府”构想。其内部宣示了高效官僚体系的美学。一踏入大楼，预设的办事者便被直接引向包覆青铜的电梯与自动扶梯，随后便被带往岗位。绿色大理石覆盖墙面，以流线型的现代装饰曲线柔和地勾勒转角。

paragraphs.en: Ben Shahn and Philip Guston, who went on to join the ranks of the most-renowned American artists of the 20th century, won plum commissions on the ground floor, as did Seymour Fogel, a respected muralist. (Ethel and Jenne Magafan, Colorado painters, collaborated on a mountain landscape for the fifth-floor boardroom.) Shahn got the most prominent assignment: two murals, each roughly 70 feet long and 24 feet high, on either side of a central corridor. One wall is a somber portrait of Americans suffering the effects of child poverty, old age, and unemployment. The other is a paean to life after Social Security: basketball games, public works, a bountiful harvest. Shahn named the murals simply The Meaning of Social Security. He considered them his best, and many critics agree.
paragraphs.zh: 本·沙恩（Ben Shahn）和菲利普·古斯顿（Philip Guston）后来都成为20世纪最知名的美国艺术家之一，他们都在一楼拿下了重要委托，受人尊敬的壁画家西摩·福格尔（Seymour Fogel）也是如此。科罗拉多画家埃塞尔和珍妮·马加凡（Ethel and Jenne Magafan）则为五层楼的董事会会议室合作创作了一幅山地风景壁画。沙恩获得了最醒目的任务：在中央走廊两侧各绘制一幅大型壁画，每幅大约长70英尺、高24英尺。 一幅是美国人因儿童贫困、老龄化和失业而受苦的忧郁写照；另一幅则是对社会保障时代生活的颂歌：篮球比赛、公共工程、丰收景象。沙恩把壁画统称为《社会保障的意义》。他认为这是自己最好的作品，许多评论者也都持同样观点。

paragraphs.en: Now, however, the building may be sold, and quite possibly demolished. If so, Shahn’s and Fogel’s murals, painted directly onto the walls, would be very hard to remove. Last year, the Cohen building was added to a list of federal properties marked for “accelerated disposition,” meaning fast-tracked sale. Living New Deal is leading the campaign to save the building, but the battle will be tough. According to sworn testimony, the Trump administration is already soliciting bids to tear down the Cohen and three other federal buildings, bypassing the usual reviews. Meanwhile, the regulations meant to protect historic buildings are being weakened.
paragraphs.zh: 然而，如今这栋楼可能被出售，并很可能被拆除。如果发生这种情况，直接涂绘在墙壁上的沙恩和福格尔壁画将几乎无法拆卸。去年，科恩大楼被列入一份联邦财产“加速处置”清单，意味着将加快出售进程。保住该建筑的运动由“活着的新政”（Living New Deal）发起，但这场战斗将十分艰难。根据宣誓证词，特朗普政府已开始征集拆除科恩大楼及另外三座联邦建筑的投标，且绕过了常规审查程序。与此同时，旨在保护历史建筑的相关法规正被削弱。

paragraphs.en: The current president is just as adept as Roosevelt was at using public space to define the state. That is why Trump razed the White House’s East Wing and plans to replace it with a huge gilded ballroom; wants to erect a triumphal arch; and has announced that his overhaul of the Kennedy Center will take the structure down to “the steel.” We don’t know whether Trump would be involved in putting up another building should the Cohen be leveled, but he is clearly not finished remaking Washington in his image.
paragraphs.zh: 现任总统在利用公共空间来界定国家形象方面，与罗斯福一样娴熟。这也是为什么特朗普拆除了白宫东翼，并计划用一座巨大的镀金舞厅取而代之；为什么他想建一座凯旋门；以及为什么他宣布对肯尼迪中心进行改造时，要把建筑拆到“钢结构”——也就是最底层骨架。我们不知道若科恩大楼被夷平，特朗普是否会亲自参与新建筑的建设，但有一点很清楚：他显然还没有完成按自己意愿改造华盛顿的计划。

paragraphs.en: Culture made by government fiat is generally labeled propaganda, especially when it promotes unpopular ideas. In a liberal society, it is an article of faith that only totalitarian regimes force artists to shape public opinion, airbrushing this, glorifying that, especially the great leader. Critics dismissed New Deal art as propaganda in its day, and for decades afterward. The complaints came from the right (the art was Soviet-adjacent) and from the left (it glossed over harsh realities and shored up capitalism). Later, criticism also came from arbiters of high modernism, such as Clement Greenberg, who wrote off New Deal art as kitsch for the masses.
paragraphs.zh: 由政府命令制造的文化作品通常会被贴上宣传品的标签，尤其是当它传播的不受欢迎观点时。在自由社会，人们普遍相信只有极权政权才会强迫艺术家塑造舆论、粉饰事实、歌颂某位伟大领袖。新政艺术在当时就被批评为宣传工具，而且这种说法持续了几十年。反对的声音既来自右翼（认为它接近苏联），也来自左翼（认为它掩盖了严酷现实并维护资本主义）。后来，批评又来自高级现代主义的权威人物，比如克莱门特·格林伯格（Clement Greenberg），他把新政艺术一概视为“给大众看的低俗艺术”。

paragraphs.en: Such sweeping judgments went too far. The huge body of work produced for arts relief programs during the Depression varied widely in quality. Among the most experienced muralists were the Cohen building artists, and the phrase social security held out untapped possibilities for invention. The gist seemed simple: By safeguarding citizens against penury, the good nation ensures that they flourish. But flourishing comes in many forms. If you’re looking for the meaning of social security, you won’t find it here.
paragraphs.zh: 这些一刀切的判断过于武断。大萧条时期为艺术救济计划创作的大量作品在质量上差异很大。在最有经验的壁画艺术家中，曾有科恩大楼的创作者，而“社会保障”这一概念也蕴含着尚未被开发的创造可能性。其核心似乎很简单：通过保障公民免于贫困，国家就能让他们茁壮成长。但茁壮成长有很多种形式。如果你想在这里寻找“社会保障”的最终含义，你是找不到的。

paragraphs.en: From the December 2020 issue: Sarah Boxer on photography and race in the Great Depression
paragraphs.zh: 摘自2020年12月号：莎拉·博克斯论大萧条时期的摄影与种族问题

paragraphs.en: I visited the Cohen building on a sunny day in February to see for myself what it had to say. Four delicate bas-reliefs in granite embellish the pediments over its four entrances. Sunlight bounced off the snow and the building’s monumental limestone facade, and the friezes almost disappeared in the glare. I paused to study one called Family Group, by Emma Lu Davis, an unusually domestic tableau to see on the exterior of a public building. A worker holding a lunch box places his free hand on the shoulder of his seated wife, as if to say goodbye for the day. She looks up at him, and the toddler at her feet looks up at her and tugs her dress, and the three form a circle. But the story doesn’t end there. They are in turn encircled by a thin line, which I realized after a moment is the profile of a man. He bows his head in thoughtful concern, and I understood that he represents the benevolent paternal state; he holds the family in his thoughts. A rather claustrophobic image, I felt, until it occurred to me that during the Depression, being held close by the government would have come as a relief. Like any historical artifact, the Cohen building demands that you enter into the spirit of the past.
paragraphs.zh: 我在一个阳光明媚的2月日子亲自去了科恩大楼，想看看它到底想表达什么。四块精致的花岗岩浅浮雕装饰着四个入口上方的山墙。阳光从雪地和大楼雄伟的灰岩立面上反射回来，连同照射的强光下，浮雕几乎消失。我停下来仔细看了看《家庭群像》（Family Group）这件作品，作者是艾玛·卢·戴维斯（Emma Lu Davis），这是一幅在公共建筑外立面上罕见的温馨家庭场景。一个工人提着午餐盒，用另一只空着的手搭在坐着的妻子的肩上，仿佛是作日常分别；妻子抬头看着他，脚边的幼儿也抬起头看着母亲，并拽着她的裙摆，三者形成一个小小的圆。可画面并未止步于此；旋即我意识到，围绕他们的是一条细线，原来是一个男人的侧影。他低头，神情里有深沉的关切，我明白他象征着慈父般仁慈的国家权威， 他在心中怀抱着这个家庭。这让我一开始觉得图像有些压抑，直到我想到，在大萧条时期，能够被政府紧紧托住反而是一种解脱。像任何历史文物一样，科恩大楼要求你走入过去的精神世界。

paragraphs.en: A postcard from the 1940s shows the Social Security Board Building shortly after its completion. (Tichnor Brothers Collection / Boston Public Library)
paragraphs.zh: 一张20世纪40年代的明信片上，展现了社会保障署大楼在竣工不久后的样貌。（Tichnor Brothers Collection / 波士顿公共图书馆）

paragraphs.en: The building in its current state was disturbing. The main entrance is now closed, so I went in through another and found myself in a cramped, dim vestibule. Still bedazzled by the bright, hopeful exterior, I felt as if I had entered a much-diminished present. The main hallways were wide but silent and empty. When I toured the upper floors, I grasped the dimensions of the emptiness. One of the building’s longtime occupants, the Department of Health and Human Services, has been gutted by the Trump administration. The other occupant, Voice of America, the federal news agency founded to combat Nazi propaganda, has been slashed by the president, who has called it “a total left-wing disaster.” All but a few of its radio programs have shut down, and those few barely operate. (A federal judge has since voided the VOA layoffs.) I opened doors into unlit newsrooms and broadcast studios. In some of them, the equipment had been ripped off the walls, leaving cut wires dangling and bits of plastic scattered across desktops and floors. Occasionally, I passed a clump of engineers and, more rarely, a lone broadcaster talking into a microphone.
paragraphs.zh: 建筑物如今的样子令人不安。主入口已被关闭，于是我从另一处进来，发现自己置身于一处狭窄阴暗的前厅。即使外墙仍然明亮、充满希望，我仍感觉像是走进了一个大幅萎缩的当下。主楼道虽然宽敞，却安静而空荡。当我参观上层时，更直观地感受到了那种空旷：这座大楼的长期租户之一，卫生与公共服务部（HHS）已被特朗普政府几乎清空。另一位租户“美国之音”则是一个由政府创立、旨在对抗纳粹宣传的联邦新闻机构，也已被总统大幅削减，他曾称其为“完全的左翼灾难”。除去少数几个项目外，它的广播项目几乎全都停摆，那些剩余的也几乎难以维持运行。（一位联邦法官此后推翻了美国之音的裁员决定。）我推开门走进一间间没有灯光的新闻编辑室和播音室。在一些房间里，墙上原有的设备被连根拔起，留下垂挂的电线和散落在桌面与地上的塑料碎片。偶尔，我会经过一小群工程师，较少见到一名播音员对着麦克风独自开口。

paragraphs.en: What sense of life remains in the building radiates from the murals on the ground floor, especially Shahn’s post–Social Security mural. In the foreground of an urban scene, young men leap into the air, vying for a basketball. They’re slightly oversize, as if almost too close to us, and their muscular heft is underscored by smaller figures playing handball in the background. Nearby, men drill the girders of a bridge; the iron beams make bold crisscrosses, moving the eye diagonally. Farther along, the frame of a house and the carpenters reaching to hammer its beams sweep the eye upward. The mural fills the entire wall, punctuated only by three doors, and Shahn cleverly integrates their lintels into his narrative. Bricklayers build a wall on top of one. In another scene above a door, a mother seems to have brought a baby and two sons to watch some construction workers. One of the sons leans on the lintel; behind them, an older man—their grandfather?—gets ready to kiss the baby.
paragraphs.zh: 这座楼里唯一尚有生命感的气息，来自一层大厅的壁画，尤其是沙恩（Shahn）的“社会保障”壁画。画面前景里，年轻男子在城市街景中跃起争抢篮球，似乎离观者有些过于接近，略带夸张地放大。背景中较小的人物在打手球，进一步强调了他们的肌肉力量。旁边，工人正在钻孔并安装一座桥梁的钢梁；粗壮的铁梁以强烈的交叉线条牵引着视线向对角线方向移动。再往前看，屋宇框架与挥舞木槌的木匠把目光向上推移。整幅壁画占满整面墙壁，只有三扇门打断了连续性，而沙恩聪明地把门楣也编入叙事之中：一个砖工在一扇门上方砌墙。另一扇门上方的场景中，一位母亲似乎带着一个婴儿和两个儿子来看几位建筑工人；其中一个男孩倚在门楣上，身后，一位上了年纪的男人——也许是他们的祖父——正准备去亲吻那个婴儿。

paragraphs.en: By contrast, the opposite wall, which shows America before Social Security, is divided into separate panels, and its figures are still. In one panel, children maimed or exhausted by labor stare out bleakly; in another, unemployed men in a small industrial town sit or stand around. This mural ought to feel static, and yet here, too, Shahn creates a sense of motion. Behind the children, we see that a door opens onto a vista of men working in a mine. In another panel, a man and child walk away from the town along a railroad track that curves up and to the left, and then disappears. The jury that chose Shahn liked his entry because of “the variety in the tempo and texture.”
paragraphs.zh: 相比之下，对面那面壁画描绘了“社会保障”之前的美国，分成了多个独立面板，人物也显得静止。在一个面板里，孩子们因伤残或劳作过度而面容憔悴、疲惫，目光黯淡地望向外界；在另一个面板里，几位失业男子在一个小工业城里坐着或站着。这样的壁画本应显得静态，但沙恩在这里同样制造出运动感。孩子身后的门敞开后，我们看到一片矿场景象，许多人在矿井里工作。另一个面板里，一名男人和一个孩子沿着铁路轨道走出城镇，轨道向左上方弯曲然后消失。评委会当年之所以选中沙恩的作品，正是因为他展现了“节奏与肌理的变化”。

paragraphs.en: Art-history encyclopedias generally associate New Deal art with Socialist Realism, a dourly idealizing style identified with Soviet communism, but it was only one influence among many. Artists took imagery and iconography from Regionalism—think Grant Wood and Thomas Hart Benton—which leaned toward heartland nativism. German Expressionism and Surrealism, deemed degenerate by Nazis and Soviets alike, show up in stylized figuration, spatial distortion, and a dreamlike atmosphere. Shahn, Philip Guston, and Seymour Fogel also learned from the masters of an older generation of revolutionary Mexican muralists. Shahn and Fogel assisted Diego Rivera on his doomed Rockefeller Center project in 1933 (the Rockefellers destroyed it when he would not remove Lenin’s face). Rivera produced monumental, socially conscious murals, but he borrowed exuberantly from other sources: Aztec art, Renaissance frescoes, Cubism, and more.
paragraphs.zh: 艺术史百科全书通常把新政时期的艺术与社会主义现实主义联系在一起，这是一种带有阴沉理想化色彩、常被视为与苏联共产主义相关的风格，但那只是在诸多影响中之一。艺术家们还借用了地区主义的图像与符号体系，例如葛兰特·伍德（Grant Wood）和托马斯·哈特·本顿（Thomas Hart Benton）的创作传统，这一传统倾向于中部心脏地带式的本土主义。德意志表现主义与超现实主义同样可见，其风格曾同时被纳粹和苏联斥为“堕落艺术”；它们在程式化的造型、空间扭曲和梦境般氛围中留下了痕迹。沙恩、菲利普·加斯顿（Philip Guston）与西摩·福格尔（Seymour Fogel）也都从一代更早的革命性墨西哥壁画大师处汲取过灵感。1933年，沙恩与福格尔曾协助迭戈·里维拉（Diego Rivera）推进其洛克菲勒中心工程（后来夭折的那个项目；当里维拉拒绝去除列宁头像时，洛克菲勒家族将其毁掉）。里维拉创作了宏大的、具有社会良知意识的壁画，但他也热情地吸收了其他来源：阿兹特克艺术、文艺复兴时期壁画、立体主义等。

paragraphs.en: The subject matter of New Deal art was more constrained and at times boosterish. According to John P. Murphy’s excellent introduction, New Deal Art, Edward Bruce, the founder of Roosevelt’s arts programs, prided himself on giving artists their freedom— as long as they eschewed abstraction, nudity, and overtly political proselytizing. What he wanted to see was “the American scene in all its phases”—city and countryside, farmers and factory workers, fields and recreational spaces, mines and railroads. The packet that Bruce assembled for the Social Security competition stressed that government support shored up the traditional family the way that pioneering families had helped one another; the effect was to associate the new policy with the American frontier, rather than radical collectivism.
paragraphs.zh: 新政艺术的题材更为受限，而且有时带有鼓吹色彩。约翰·P·墨菲在《新政艺术》里的精彩导言中写道，罗斯福艺术项目的创始人爱德华·布鲁斯引以为豪的是让艺术家保持创作自由——只要他们避开抽象、裸露和公开的政治宣传。他想看到的是“美国景象的全部阶段”——城市与乡村、农夫与工厂工人、田野与休闲空间、矿山与铁路。布鲁斯为“社会保障”比赛整理的文档强调，政府支持能够像先驱家庭互相帮助那样支撑起传统家庭；这样做的效果是把这项新政策与美国边疆精神联系在一起，而不是与激进的集体主义相勾连。

paragraphs.en: From the August 1935 issue: Edward Bruce on art and democracy
paragraphs.zh: 1935年8月号：爱德华·布鲁斯谈艺术与民主

paragraphs.en: Shahn more or less ignored the hint. The only family in his murals was the one squeezed atop the lintel. His interest lay in society and its diversity—a very familiar notion today, but not back then. Murals commissioned by the Section of Fine Arts (especially in the South) tended to shy away from the realities of Black life, and Black people were often shown in subservient roles. Shahn didn’t make a big point of integration, but his people are Black and brown as well as white. Their bodies are squat and bulky, tall and alarmingly skinny. The beneficiaries of Social Security don’t exhibit fake cheer—they aren’t sad, but they don’t smile, either; they are intent on their work. Those still in need of assistance are grim but have their dignity. In a nice touch by Shahn, a swarthy man in a fedora who assumes a proud, defiant stance—his arms are crossed and he scowls, as if to say, “Show me!”—appears in both murals unchanged. In the before scene, he waits in a line of unemployed men. In the after, he waits in a line of men signing up for Social Security. He’s a skeptic; he’ll wait and see. That’s his right, and Shahn respects it.
paragraphs.zh: 肖恩几乎把这个提示置之不理。他的壁画里唯一出现的家庭，是挤在门楣上的那一个。与此相比，他更关注的是社会及其多样性——这个观念在今天看来很熟悉，但当年并不常见。由美术委员会（尤其在南方）委托的壁画往往回避黑人生活的现实，黑人常被置于从属角色。肖恩并未把种族融合当作突出主题，但他的人物既有黑人和褐色人群，也有白人。人物身形有的矮壮、有的高挑而又出奇地瘦。社会保障的受益者并不装出假笑——他们并不悲伤，也不一定微笑；他们专注于自己的工作。仍在求助中的人虽严峻，却保持着尊严。肖恩的一个细节很妙：一位肤色黝黑、戴费多拉帽、摆出骄傲而倔强姿态的男人——双臂交叉、面露不满，仿佛在说“拿来吧！”——在两幅壁画中毫无变化地出现。在“前”场景中，他站在失业男性的队列里；在“后”场景中，他又站在登记社会保障的男性队伍里。他是个怀疑者；他会等着看结果。这是他的权利，肖恩也尊重这一点。

paragraphs.en: Shahn’s realism extended to noting Social Security’s flaws. To win votes from Southern Democrats, Roosevelt had agreed to deny Social Security to agricultural and domestic laborers—excluding a large percentage of Black workers. Laura Katzman, an art-history professor at James Madison University and the curator of a recent Shahn retrospective whom I spoke with, pointed out that Shahn made sure to include farmworkers and a caregiver in his cast of characters. In one of the pre–Social Security panels, a woman holding a child “looks Latino” and is probably a nanny, Katzman said, observing that “the child is very blue-eyed” and has red hair. I noticed later that the woman’s white dress resembles a maid’s uniform.
paragraphs.zh: 肖恩的写实主义也延展到对社会保障缺陷的揭示。为赢得南方民主党人的选票，罗斯福同意将农业和家政劳动者排除在社会保障体系之外，从而把大量黑人劳动者排除在外。詹姆斯·麦迪逊大学的艺术史教授、最近一次肖恩回顾展主办方的策展人劳拉·卡茨曼（我与她交流过）指出，肖恩在人物群像中专门加入了农场工人和一名看护者。在“社会保障之前”的其中一块壁画上，一位抱着孩子的女性“看起来像拉丁裔”，很可能是保姆；卡茨曼说，那名孩子“眼睛很蓝”，而且有红色头发。我后来注意到，这位女性的白色连衣裙很像女佣制服。

paragraphs.en: Fogel’s murals, two frescoes just inside the original main entrance, weave a streamlined Futurism and bright Mexican Modernism into their Socialist Realism. Wealth of the Nation depicts a utopia peopled by broad-shouldered heroes of the new economy—a scientist, an architect, two construction workers, and, in the background, a giant, half-naked proletarian, back muscles popping as he strains to pull the lever for two huge cogwheels. Security of the People demonstrates what the wealth of the nation is for: to provide respite to the nuclear family, shown outdoors engaged in leisure activities.
paragraphs.zh: 福格尔的壁画——两幅位于原正门内侧的湿壁画——将流线型未来主义和明亮的墨西哥现代主义融入其社会主义现实主义叙事中。《民族财富》描绘了一个乌托邦场景：由一位科学家、一位建筑师、两名建筑工人，以及背景中一位巨大的半裸无产阶级工人构成；后者后背肌肉绷紧，正用力拉动巨大的杠杆，驱动两只巨型齿轮。《人民安全》展示了《民族财富》的用途：为核家庭提供身处户外从事休闲活动时的喘息空间。

paragraphs.en: On further inspection, though, the mood changes. In both murals, each figure occupies a separate space. Nobody looks at anybody else, with one exception: In Security of the People, a woman holding a naked child gazes balefully at a man immersed in his newspaper. In early studies for the project, Fogel had depicted Dickensian insecurity (a starving woman, homeless men, a punishing workplace). The dystopian gloom seems to have infected the new Eden. Perhaps Fogel, a fervent leftist, chafed at having to celebrate the family, that bourgeois institution.
paragraphs.zh: 但进一步观察时，氛围便不同了。在这两幅壁画里，每个人物都占据独立空间。除了一处例外，没有人看向其他人：在《人民安全》中，一位抱着赤裸孩子的女人冷冷地注视着一名埋头看报纸的男子。早期的创作草图中，福格尔曾描绘过《狄更斯式》的不安——有一个挨饿的女人、无家可归的男人、一间带有惩罚意味的工作场所。那种反乌托邦般的阴郁似乎已污染了这个“新伊甸园”。或许福格尔这位热情的左翼主义者，对不得不去歌颂家庭这一本质上是资产阶级制度的机构感到不满。

paragraphs.en: Philip Guston’s triptych, evoking the Last Supper and a Madonna and Christ child, has the aura of an altarpiece in the Cohen building’s auditorium. (U.S. General Services Administration)
paragraphs.zh: 菲利普·加斯顿的三联画借用了《最后的晚餐》和圣母与基督童子的意象，在科恩大楼的礼堂里拥有像祭坛画般的气质。（美国总务管理局）

paragraphs.en: Guston’s Reconstruction and the Well-Being of the Family—a triptych on wood panels—is mounted on the stage of the auditorium and dominates the hall like an altarpiece in a nave. The painting is erudite; it packs in allusions. The father stretches out his arms, Jesus-like, evoking the Last Supper, and the mother holds a toddler on her lap, face forward, the way the Madonna might present the Christ child. The side panels show men at work, one digging in a surreal desert, two breaking Cubist-looking rocks. All of the figures have elongated limbs, Picasso-style, and they gaze into the middle distance like early-Renaissance saints (Piero della Francesca was one of Guston’s favorite painters). In the early 1940s, as Guston was painting this mural, he had begun to turn away from political art to introspective easel painting, and one wonders whether the religious references idealize the New Deal family or half-mock its idealization.
paragraphs.zh: 加斯顿的《重建与家庭福祉》——一幅木板三联画——被安装在礼堂舞台上，如同教堂中殿堂一侧的祭坛画般主导全场。这幅作品学养深厚，充满引文与致意。画中父亲双臂向外伸展，姿态近似耶稣，唤起《最后的晚餐》的记忆；母亲坐在一把小椅上抱着一个蹒跚坐在腿上的幼儿，正面注视观者，彷佛圣母在展现基督童子。边饰画面里，男子在劳动：一人在超现实的荒漠中挖掘，两人在劈开宛若立体主义的岩石。所有人物都像毕加索笔下那样四肢修长，目光都投向中景，仿佛文艺复兴早期圣徒（皮耶罗·德拉·弗朗切斯卡是加斯顿最喜爱的画家之一）。在1940年代初，加斯顿创作这幅壁画之时，已开始从政治性艺术转向更具内省性的写实创作，于是人们不禁思考：他这些宗教化的暗示是理想化了新政家庭，还是半带讽刺地戏仿了这种理想化。

paragraphs.en: Compared with the other muralists in the Cohen building, Shahn comes off as the ultimate New Deal artist. He is the least subversive, the most sincere. And yet his murals have aged the best. His individuals are truly individual; they have an unquenchable vitality and collectively convey an adamant humanism, a subversive ideology in its own right. Shahn had worked for an art journal with Communist Party ties in the early 1930s, but he turned against the party after Stalin’s show trials and pact with Hitler later in the decade. And Shahn had just spent three years working as a photographer for the Resettlement Administration, later the Farm Security Administration, traveling around the country in the company of Walker Evans, Dorothea Lange, and others to document the American people. The job changed both Shahn’s politics and his paintings.
paragraphs.zh: 与科恩大楼里其他壁画家相比，沙恩更像是最典型的新政艺术家。他最不具颠覆性，也最真诚。可他的壁画却是保存得最好的。作品中的人物都是真正的“个体”；它们有着不竭的生命力，并且共同传达出坚定的人道主义，这本身就构成了一种颠覆性的思想立场。沙恩在20世纪30年代初曾为一份与共产党有关联的艺术杂志工作，但后来在20世纪30年代后期在斯大林的公开审判和与希特勒签订条约后，他转而反对共产党。随后沙恩又连续三年在“移民复安局”（后来改为农场安全署）担任摄影师，与沃克·埃文斯、黛洛西亚·兰格等人一起在全国各地旅行，记录美国人民的生活。这项工作改变了沙恩的政治立场，也改变了他的绘画。

paragraphs.en: The trips brought him into contact with the particulars of a reality that was beyond his imagination. He met idiosyncratic characters he couldn’t have made up, and saw suffering he hadn’t understood before—perhaps because he had prioritized political theory over direct experience. “Everything I had gotten about the condition of miners or cotton pickers I’d gotten on Fourteenth Street,” he said in a 1965 interview, presumably alluding to his own neighborhood, New York’s Greenwich Village, then home to many radicals. Photography made him abandon Social Realism for what he called “personal realism,” he wrote in his book The Shape of Content.
paragraphs.zh: 这些行程让他接触到前所未见的现实细节。那些他无法凭空想象出的独特人物出现在他面前，他也看到了此前未曾理解的苦难——也许因为他先前过于重视政治理论，而非直接经验。沙恩在1965年的一次采访中说：“我对矿工和采棉工人状况的了解，都是在第十四街上学来的。”他指的很可能是他自己的社区——当时美国纽约格林威治村，许多激进分子都居住在那里。正是摄影促使他放弃了社会现实主义，转向他在《内容的形态》中所说的“个人现实主义”。

paragraphs.en: From the September 1957 issue: Ben Shahn on nonconformity
paragraphs.zh: 1957年9月刊：贝恩·沙恩谈非从众主义

paragraphs.en: Shahn had a radical’s take on political art, though. He refused to accept that art is not art if it is propaganda. All that claim meant, he said, is that the art had social content. And even if it was intended to persuade, that didn’t make it bad. “Propaganda is to me a noble word,” Shahn said in a 1968 oral history. “It means you believe something very strongly and you want other people to believe it; you want to propagate your faith.” No one claims that European art was the worse for having propounded Christian theology, he continued. “When Giotto did the frescoes in Assisi, you know, that was as propagandistic a work of art as there was.”
paragraphs.zh: 然而，沙恩对政治性艺术有着激进的观点。他拒绝接受“只要是宣传就不算艺术”这一说法。对此的全部解释，在他看来，就是作品带有社会内容而已。即使作品带有劝导性，也不意味着它就不够好。沙恩在1968年的一段口述史中说：“在我看来，宣传是个高尚的词。”意思是，你深信某件事，并希望别人也相信；你想把你的信念传播出去。接着他说，没有人会说欧洲艺术因为传播基督教神学而变得更糟。“当吉奥托在阿西西作壁画时，你知道吗，那绝对是有史以来最具宣传性的艺术作品之一。”

paragraphs.en: By 1940, when the Social Security competition took place, the New Deal was already coming to an end. Shahn and his partner, the painter Bernarda Bryson, who collaborated with him on several projects, set out for Washington to begin the murals on December 8, 1941—one day after Pearl Harbor. Instead of the Social Security Board, the War Department moved into the building, which got so crowded that desks filled the corridor where Shahn was painting. Conservators later found burn marks on the walls where people had stubbed out their cigarettes. Perhaps the air of gravitas evident in all the building’s murals, and the ambivalence hinted at in Guston’s and Fogel’s, reflects the anxieties of the time. While Shahn was working, people came up to him and said that what he was painting was what they were fighting for.
paragraphs.zh: 到1940年，社会保障竞赛进行时，“新政”已经走向尾声。肖恩（Shahn）与合作者、画家伯纳达·布莱森（Bernarda Bryson）一同前往华盛顿，准备从12月8日、也就是珍珠港事件后一天开始着手壁画创作。社会保障委员会并未最终进驻，战争部却挤进了该大楼，屋内挤得十分拥挤，肖恩作画时，走廊里都被办公桌占满。后来，修复人员在墙上发现了烟头熄灭后留下的焦痕。或许正因为这栋建筑内所有壁画都带有一种庄重的气质，再加上古斯顿（Guston）和福格尔（Fogel）作品中若隐若现的矛盾感，这正反映了当时人们的焦虑。肖恩作画期间，不少人走来告诉他：他正在画的，就是他们正在为之而战的东西。

paragraphs.en: Over the course of the early ’40s, funding for New Deal arts programs dried up. Shahn’s The Meaning of Social Security, completed in 1942, were his last murals for the Roosevelt administration. The painters who became Abstract Expressionists—many of them former New Deal artists themselves—rejected the sentimentality of “American scene” art. Then again, they did their work after the war, when democracy seemed to be winning the day. Eight decades later, as nations inch toward despotism, an art animated by democratic impulses makes a stronger case for itself. It proposes a mutual bond between Americans and their government—a vision of the security we are due and the loyalty we owe in return. That reciprocity remains very much an unfinished project. Right now, government is being torn down, rather than built up. If the Cohen building is not demolished, it could remind future generations what patriotism looked like.
paragraphs.zh: 在40年代初期，“新政”艺术项目的经费陆续枯竭。肖恩于1942年完成的《社会保障的意义》，是他为罗斯福政府所绘制的最后一组壁画。后来成为抽象表现主义者的一批画家——其中许多人本身就是前“新政”艺术家——拒绝了“美国场景”艺术中的煽情倾向。再者说，他们是在战后做这些工作的，当时民主似乎已经赢得了胜利。八十年后，随着各国走向专制的步伐加快，一种由民主情绪驱动的艺术更有力地为自己辩护。它提出了美国人与政府之间的一种互惠关系：一幅我们应当获得的安全保障图景，以及我们应回馈的忠诚。那种互惠关系至今仍是一个远未完成的工程。如今，政府正在被拆解，而不是被建构。如果科恩大楼没有被拆除，它仍能提醒后世，真正的爱国情怀曾是什么样子。

paragraphs.en: This article appears in the May 2026 print edition with the headline “The Art of the (New) Deal.” When you buy a book using a link on this page, we receive a commission. Thank you for supporting The Atlantic.
paragraphs.zh: 这篇文章发表于2026年5月号纸质版，标题是《（新）政的艺术》。当你通过本页的链接购买图书时，我们会获得一笔佣金。感谢你对《大西洋月刊》的支持。
